content.problemStatement:
You are given NNN pairs of integers. The iii-th pair is denoted (Ai,Bi)(A_i, B_i)(Ai‚Äã,Bi‚Äã).

It's guaranteed that Ai‚â§BiA_i \le B_iAi‚Äã‚â§Bi‚Äã for every 1‚â§i‚â§N1 \le i \le N1‚â§i‚â§N.

For an integer XXX, we define f(X)f(X)f(X) to be the result of the following process:


Let YYY be a variable.

Initially, Y=XY = XY=X.
For each i=1,2,‚Ä¶,Ni = 1, 2, \ldots, Ni=1,2,‚Ä¶,N **in order**,

If Y<AiY \lt A_iY<Ai‚Äã, set Y=AiY = A_iY=Ai‚Äã.
If Y>BiY \gt B_iY>Bi‚Äã, set Y=BiY = B_iY=Bi‚Äã.
If Ai‚â§Y‚â§BiA_i \le Y \le B_iAi‚Äã‚â§Y‚â§Bi‚Äã, the value of YYY doesn't change.


f(X)f(X)f(X) is defined to be the final value of YYY after all NNN steps of the process have been performed.

Your task is to find the **maximum possible value** of f(X)f(X)f(X) across all possible input integers XXX.

It can be proved that this value is finite.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚Äî the number of pairs of integers.
The next NNN lines describe the pairs. The iii-th of these NNN lines contains two space-separated integers AiA_iAi‚Äã and BiB_iBi‚Äã, denoting the elements of the iii-th pair.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the maximum possible value of f(X)f(X)f(X) across all integers XXX.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Consider choosing X=9X = 9X=9. Then, f(X)f(X)f(X) will be computed as follows:

We start with Y=9Y = 9Y=9.
At i=1i = 1i=1, we have Y>B1=5Y \gt B_1 = 5Y>B1‚Äã=5.
So, we set Y=B1=5Y = B_1 = 5Y=B1‚Äã=5.
At i=2i = 2i=2, we now have A2‚â§Y‚â§B2A_2 \le Y \le B_2A2‚Äã‚â§Y‚â§B2‚Äã because A2=2A_2 = 2A2‚Äã=2 and B2=6B_2 = 6B2‚Äã=6.
So, YYY remains unchanged.

The final value of YYY is 555, and so f(9)=5f(9) = 5f(9)=5.
It can be proved that this is the largest possible value of f(X)f(X)f(X) for any input XXX.
Test case 222: Because A3=B3=2A_3 = B_3 = 2A3‚Äã=B3‚Äã=2, no matter which XXX we choose we will always end up with f(X)=2f(X) = 2f(X)=2.
So, the answer is 222.
Test case 333: One possible optimal choice of XXX is X=1X = 1X=1, which can be verified to give f(X)=7f(X) = 7f(X)=7.
No other XXX results in a larger f(X)f(X)f(X), so the answer is 777.
